class_schedule.topic: Financial Markets, Asset Classes, and the Investment Landscape
learning_objectives: Identify the major asset classes (equities, fixed income, derivatives, commodities, real estate, alternatives) and their defining characteristics
Explain the structure of primary and secondary markets, exchange-traded versus OTC trading, and the role of intermediaries
Distinguish among the major participants in financial markets: households, institutions, governments, and intermediaries
Describe the historical risk and return profile of stocks, bonds, and bills using long-run U.S. data (Ibbotson, Siegel)
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 1-2 - The Investment Environment and Asset Classes
Malkiel, A Random Walk Down Wall Street (13th ed.), Ch. 1 - Firm Foundations and Castles in the Air
Siegel, Stocks for the Long Run (6th ed.), Ch. 1-2 - Long-term historical returns by asset class

lecture_notes.introduction: Every dollar of household wealth, every pension benefit, every endowment payout, and every corporate capital expenditure flows through the financial markets. In the United States alone, household financial assets exceed $115 trillion, the equity market capitalization of the Russell 3000 represents almost the entire investable U.S. stock universe, the U.S. Treasury market is the deepest sovereign debt market on earth, and the alternatives industry manages more than $13 trillion. Investment and Portfolio Management is the discipline of allocating capital across this opportunity set in a way that maximizes risk-adjusted return for a specific objective and constraint set. Over the next fifteen weeks you will learn how Markowitz, Sharpe, Fama, French, and Kahneman built the intellectual framework that governs how trillions of dollars are managed today, and you will learn how that framework breaks down at the edges — in the 1998 collapse of Long-Term Capital Management, in the 2008 financial crisis, in the 2021 GameStop short squeeze, in the 2021 Archegos blow-up. We start today with the landscape itself: what assets exist, how they trade, who participates, and what they have actually returned over the last century. Without that grounding the math we build later will not connect to the real economy it is meant to describe.
key_concepts: Asset class taxonomy: equities (common, preferred, ADRs), fixed income (Treasuries, agencies, munis, IG and HY corporates, securitized), derivatives (futures, forwards, options, swaps), commodities (energy, metals, agriculture), real estate (direct, REITs), and alternatives (hedge funds, private equity, venture capital, infrastructure)

Primary market: where new securities are issued (IPOs, SEOs, bond issuance via syndicates and book-building); secondary market: where existing securities trade among investors and the issuer receives no proceeds

Exchange-traded versus over-the-counter (OTC): NYSE and Nasdaq are exchanges with central order books; the U.S. Treasury, corporate bond, and most derivatives markets are dealer-intermediated OTC markets

Market participants: households, pension funds (DB and DC), mutual funds and ETFs, hedge funds, sovereign wealth funds, insurance companies, endowments and foundations, central banks, and broker-dealers
Long-run historical returns (Ibbotson SBBI, 1926-2023, U.S.): large-cap stocks ~10.2% nominal, long Treasuries ~5.5%, T-bills ~3.3%, inflation ~2.9%; the equity risk premium has averaged 5-7%

Real versus nominal returns: a 10% nominal return with 3% inflation is a 6.8% real return ((1.10/1.03) - 1); long-horizon investors should think in real terms because purchasing power is what funds retirement

Liquidity spectrum: cash and Treasuries are the most liquid; private equity, direct real estate, and venture capital are illiquid and require an illiquidity premium to compensate investors for lock-up

Direct versus indirect investing: buying individual stocks versus buying mutual funds, ETFs, separately managed accounts, or commingled vehicles; each has different cost, tax, and operational characteristics
Globalization of markets: U.S. equities are roughly 60% of global market cap (MSCI ACWI), making home-country bias a measurable phenomenon for U.S. investors who often hold 80%+ U.S. stocks

Regulatory framework: SEC (1934 Act for trading, 1933 Act for issuance), FINRA for broker-dealers, CFTC for futures, Investment Company Act of 1940 governing mutual funds, and Investment Advisers Act of 1940 governing fiduciary advice
lecture_notes.demonstration: Pull up the SPDR sector ETFs (XLK, XLF, XLE, XLV), the iShares 20+ Year Treasury ETF (TLT), the SPDR Gold Shares (GLD), and the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) on a financial data terminal. Plot rolling 12-month returns over 2000-2024 and observe how they comove during normal periods and decouple during crises (2008, 2020, 2022). Then load the Ibbotson SBBI long-run return series and compute the geometric mean and standard deviation of large-cap stocks, long-term Treasuries, and T-bills over rolling 30-year windows to show that the equity premium has been positive in every 30-year window since 1926.
setup_requirements: Bloomberg Terminal access or Yahoo Finance / FRED data downloads for historical price series
Excel or Python (pandas) for return calculations and rolling windows
Ibbotson SBBI data summary or a comparable long-run return source
Course syllabus and reading list distributed in advance

review.value: Brinson, Hood, and Beebower's classic 1986 study found that asset allocation explains roughly 90% of the variability of returns across pension funds; security selection and market timing explain the rest. The decision of how much to put in stocks versus bonds matters far more than which specific stocks you pick

The equity risk premium is the expected excess return of stocks over a risk-free asset; it has averaged 5-7% historically, but estimates of the forward-looking premium are much lower (Damodaran, Fernandez surveys put it around 4-5% in 2024). Forward and historical premia are different things

Illiquid assets must offer higher expected returns to compensate for lock-up; this is the illiquidity premium. Private equity reports of 'higher returns than public equity' often shrink to zero after adjusting for leverage, fees, and the illiquidity premium

Always state long-horizon return assumptions in real terms. A 'safe 5% bond yield' in a 4% inflation environment is barely keeping pace with purchasing power, while a 5% real return doubles purchasing power in roughly 14 years (rule of 72)

learning_objectives: Compute arithmetic and geometric mean returns and explain when each is the correct measure
Calculate variance, standard deviation, covariance, and correlation for asset returns
Construct a histogram and compute skewness and kurtosis to assess the shape of a return distribution
Distinguish between systematic (market) risk and idiosyncratic (firm-specific) risk
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 5 - Risk, Return, and the Historical Record
Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Essentials of Investments, Ch. 5
Malkiel, A Random Walk Down Wall Street (13th ed.), Ch. 8 - Modern Portfolio Theory

lecture_notes.introduction: Return is easy: you put in $100, you get out $110, you made 10%. Risk is harder. Risk is not the loss you experienced — that is realized loss — risk is the dispersion of possible outcomes around your expectation, and it must be measured before the outcome is known. The discipline of investment management lives or dies on your ability to quantify risk in a way that survives contact with reality. This session is the bridge between intuitive notions of 'this stock is risky' and the formal machinery (variance, covariance, correlation, beta) that the entire field of portfolio theory rests on. We will work through the difference between arithmetic and geometric means — a difference that has cost real investors enormous sums when reported the wrong way — and we will see why a normal distribution is a useful but incomplete model for stock returns. By the end of class you will be able to look at a fund's reported 'average annual return' and ask the right follow-up questions: arithmetic or geometric? Over what window? With what volatility? With what tail behavior? These are the questions that separate sophisticated capital allocators from retail investors who get sold simple stories.
key_concepts: Arithmetic mean return: simple average of period returns; the unbiased estimate of next period's expected return given i.i.d. returns
Geometric mean return: ((1+r1)(1+r2)...(1+rn))^(1/n) - 1; the actual compound growth rate of $1 invested over the period; always less than or equal to the arithmetic mean (Jensen's inequality)

Volatility drag: geometric mean is approximately arithmetic mean minus variance/2 for small returns; a portfolio with arithmetic mean 10% and standard deviation 20% has geometric mean around 8%, a 2-percentage-point drag
Variance: E[(R - E[R])^2], the expected squared deviation from the mean; standard deviation is the square root, expressed in the same units as the return
Covariance: E[(Ra - E[Ra])(Rb - E[Rb])]; positive means assets tend to move together; correlation is covariance divided by the product of standard deviations and is bounded by [-1, +1]
Sample versus population statistics: with n observations, divide variance by (n-1) for an unbiased sample estimator; this matters when n is small
Skewness: third standardized moment; positive skew means a long right tail, negative skew means a long left tail; equity index returns have mild negative skew

Kurtosis: fourth standardized moment; high kurtosis (fat tails) means extreme outcomes are more likely than a normal distribution would predict; daily stock returns have kurtosis of 5-10 versus 3 for a normal distribution
Systematic risk: risk that affects the whole market and cannot be diversified away (recessions, interest rates, inflation); idiosyncratic risk: firm-specific risk that diversification eliminates
Sharpe ratio (preview): excess return per unit of total risk, (E[R] - Rf) / sigma; the foundational measure of risk-adjusted return that we will use throughout the course
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a five-year monthly return series for the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and a single stock such as Tesla (TSLA). Compute arithmetic and geometric monthly means, annualize them (mean * 12 for arithmetic, (1+geom)^12 - 1 for geometric, sigma * sqrt(12) for volatility under i.i.d. assumption), and observe the volatility drag explicitly. Then compute the covariance and correlation between SPY and TSLA, and note that correlations spike during the March 2020 COVID drawdown — a phenomenon called correlation breakdown that destroys diversification exactly when investors need it most.
setup_requirements: Excel or Python with monthly return downloads for SPY, TSLA, and a Treasury ETF (IEF)
Statistical functions: AVERAGE, GEOMEAN, STDEV.S, COVARIANCE.S, CORREL in Excel or numpy/pandas in Python
Histogram-plotting capability for return distribution visualization
Reference table of normal distribution properties for comparison

review.value: Arithmetic mean overstates compound growth. A fund that returns +50% then -50% has arithmetic mean 0% but a $1 investment is worth $0.75 at the end (geometric mean -13.4%). Always use geometric mean to describe historical performance

Two assets with correlation 0.8 still provide some diversification benefit, but only assets with correlation less than 1 reduce portfolio variance below the weighted average of individual variances. Correlations close to 1 mean you do not actually have a diversified portfolio

Stock returns have kurtosis of 5-10, far above the normal distribution's 3. A '20-sigma event' under the normal model (probability near 10^-89) actually happens every few years. This is why VaR models calibrated to normal returns systematically understate tail risk

If returns are i.i.d., monthly standard deviation annualizes by multiplying by sqrt(12); daily by sqrt(252). This square-root rule is wrong if returns have autocorrelation (mean reversion or momentum), which they do at some horizons

class_schedule.topic: Modern Portfolio Theory and the Efficient Frontier
learning_objectives: Compute portfolio expected return and variance for two-asset and n-asset portfolios
Derive the efficient frontier and identify the minimum-variance and tangency portfolios
Show how diversification reduces unsystematic risk as the number of assets increases
Apply the two-fund separation theorem to construct optimal portfolios using a risky and risk-free asset
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 6-7 - Capital Allocation and Optimal Risky Portfolios
Markowitz, 'Portfolio Selection' (Journal of Finance, 1952) - the original paper
Malkiel, A Random Walk Down Wall Street (13th ed.), Ch. 8 - Modern Portfolio Theory in practice

lecture_notes.introduction: Harry Markowitz was a 25-year-old graduate student at the University of Chicago in 1952 when he wrote a 14-page paper that earned him the Nobel Prize 38 years later and reorganized the entire field of investment management. Before Markowitz, picking stocks was about finding the 'best' security — the one with the highest expected return. After Markowitz, picking stocks was about constructing portfolios where the interactions between securities matter as much as the securities themselves. The mathematics of portfolio variance shows that combining imperfectly correlated assets reduces total risk in a way that no single asset can replicate, and that for any target expected return there is one portfolio that achieves it with minimum variance. This locus of minimum-variance portfolios is the efficient frontier, and every rational mean-variance investor must choose a portfolio on it. Markowitz's framework is the foundation of every robo-advisor, every pension fund's strategic asset allocation, every endowment's policy portfolio, and every target-date retirement fund. We will work through the math today with two assets first (so the geometry is visible) and then generalize to n assets, where a simple matrix formula gives the optimal weights. By the end of class you will be able to compute the variance of a portfolio of arbitrary size and explain to a client why diversification is the only free lunch in investing.
key_concepts: Two-asset portfolio expected return: E[Rp] = w1*E[R1] + w2*E[R2], a linear combination of individual expected returns
Two-asset portfolio variance: sigma_p^2 = w1^2*sigma1^2 + w2^2*sigma2^2 + 2*w1*w2*cov(R1,R2); the covariance term creates diversification benefit when correlation is less than 1
N-asset portfolio variance in matrix form: sigma_p^2 = w' * Sigma * w, where Sigma is the n x n covariance matrix and w is the vector of weights

Diversification arithmetic: with n equally weighted assets each with variance sigma^2 and average pairwise correlation rho, portfolio variance approaches rho * sigma^2 as n grows, the systematic risk floor
Efficient frontier: the set of portfolios that maximize expected return for each level of variance; in two-asset space it is a hyperbola in (sigma, E[R]) coordinates
Minimum-variance portfolio: the leftmost point on the efficient frontier; for two assets, w1_mvp = (sigma2^2 - cov)/(sigma1^2 + sigma2^2 - 2*cov)
Tangency portfolio: the portfolio on the efficient frontier with the highest Sharpe ratio; the line from the risk-free rate tangent to the efficient frontier identifies it

Capital allocation line: the straight line in (sigma, E[R]) space from the risk-free asset through the tangency portfolio; combinations of risk-free asset and tangency portfolio dominate all other portfolios

Two-fund separation theorem: every mean-variance investor holds the same risky portfolio (the tangency portfolio) and varies only the proportion of wealth in the risk-free asset based on risk tolerance

Practical estimation problem: portfolio optimization is sensitive to expected return inputs; small changes in expected returns produce wildly different optimal weights, leading to 'corner solutions' that practitioners distrust
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a two-asset portfolio of SPY (E[R]=10%, sigma=16%) and IEF (intermediate Treasuries, E[R]=4%, sigma=6%) with correlation 0.05. Construct the efficient frontier by varying SPY weight from 0 to 100% in 5% increments and computing E[Rp] and sigma_p for each. Plot the frontier in Excel. Find the minimum-variance portfolio (w_SPY near 0.13, sigma_p near 5.9%) and the tangency portfolio assuming Rf=2% (compute Sharpe ratio at each weight, find the maximum at w_SPY near 0.62, Sharpe near 0.71). Then add a third asset (gold, GLD) and show how the frontier shifts left.
setup_requirements: Excel with covariance/correlation inputs for SPY, IEF, GLD over a five-year sample
Solver add-in for optimization or manual grid-search over weights
Plot capability for the efficient frontier in (sigma, E[R]) space
Reference Markowitz paper from JSTOR for original derivation

review.value: Combining assets with correlation less than 1 produces a portfolio whose risk is less than the weighted average of individual risks while expected return is exactly the weighted average; this is the only 'free lunch' in finance, identified mathematically by Markowitz

With average pairwise correlation 0.3, an equally weighted portfolio of 30 stocks captures most of the diversification benefit; going from 30 to 100 stocks reduces risk only marginally. This is why active managers can claim '30-50 stocks is sufficient diversification'

The tangency portfolio is the portfolio with the highest Sharpe ratio on the efficient frontier; every investor should hold this same risky portfolio and combine it with cash/borrowing to reach their preferred risk level. This is the two-fund separation theorem

Mean-variance optimization is brittle: small estimation errors in expected returns produce extreme corner allocations. Practitioners impose constraints (no shorting, max position size, sector limits) and use techniques like Black-Litterman to stabilize the optimizer's output

learning_objectives: State the CAPM equation and identify each component (risk-free rate, beta, market risk premium)
Compute beta from a regression of asset excess returns on market excess returns
Use CAPM to estimate the cost of equity for a public company
Critique the CAPM's empirical and theoretical limitations
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 9 - The Capital Asset Pricing Model
Sharpe, 'Capital Asset Prices: A Theory of Market Equilibrium under Conditions of Risk' (Journal of Finance, 1964)
Fama & French, 'The Capital Asset Pricing Model: Theory and Evidence' (Journal of Economic Perspectives, 2004)

lecture_notes.introduction: If Markowitz gave us the math of portfolio choice for a single investor, William Sharpe asked the next question: if every investor holds a Markowitz-efficient portfolio, what does the market look like in equilibrium? His 1964 answer became the Capital Asset Pricing Model, the most influential equation in finance after Black-Scholes. CAPM says that in equilibrium the expected return on any asset equals the risk-free rate plus the asset's beta times the market risk premium. Beta is the only risk that gets compensated, because all idiosyncratic risk has been diversified away. CAPM is taught in every MBA program, used in every corporate cost-of-capital calculation, embedded in every regulatory rate case for utilities, and applied in every fairness opinion for an M&A transaction. It is also empirically wrong in important ways — small stocks earn more than CAPM predicts, value stocks earn more than CAPM predicts, low-volatility stocks earn more than CAPM predicts — and the field has spent six decades extending it. But the framework remains the lingua franca of investment management because its single intuition is so powerful: only non-diversifiable risk earns a premium. Today we derive CAPM, estimate beta from real data, and confront the gap between theory and evidence.
key_concepts: CAPM equation: E[Ri] = Rf + beta_i * (E[Rm] - Rf), where Rf is the risk-free rate, E[Rm] is the expected market return, and beta_i measures asset i's sensitivity to market movements
Beta definition: beta_i = cov(Ri, Rm) / var(Rm); the slope coefficient from regressing asset i's excess returns on market excess returns
Market portfolio: in CAPM, the market is the value-weighted portfolio of all risky assets; in practice, the S&P 500 or a global equity index is used as a proxy
Security market line (SML): a graph of expected return versus beta with slope equal to the market risk premium and intercept equal to the risk-free rate
Beta estimation: typically run on 60 months of data using a regression of (Ri - Rf) on (Rm - Rf); high R-squared indicates the market explains most of the variation

Beta interpretation: beta = 1 means the asset moves with the market; beta = 1.5 means it amplifies market moves by 50%; beta = 0.5 means it dampens market moves by half; beta less than 0 means it moves opposite to the market
Cost of equity application: corporate finance uses CAPM to set the discount rate for cash flow valuation; for Apple in 2024 with beta near 1.2, Rf=4.3%, MRP=5%, cost of equity near 10.3%

CAPM assumptions: investors are mean-variance optimizers, all share the same expectations, borrowing and lending at the risk-free rate, no taxes or transaction costs, the market portfolio is observable
Empirical anomalies: size effect (small caps earn excess returns), value effect (high B/M stocks outperform), momentum (past winners continue), low-volatility anomaly

Practical adjustments: Blume adjustment (beta = 0.67*raw_beta + 0.33*1.0) shrinks beta toward 1 for stability; industry betas are sometimes used in place of company-specific betas for early-stage firms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Download 60 months of monthly returns for Apple (AAPL), JPMorgan (JPM), Walmart (WMT), and a long Treasury (TLT) along with the S&P 500 and the 1-month T-bill rate. Compute monthly excess returns for each. Run a linear regression of each asset's excess return on the market excess return. Apple beta near 1.2, JPM beta near 1.1, Walmart beta near 0.5, TLT beta near -0.3. Use CAPM with Rf=4.3% and market risk premium 5% to compute expected returns. Compare to historical means and discuss the gap.
setup_requirements: Monthly return data for AAPL, JPM, WMT, TLT, SPY, and 1-month T-bill from FRED or Yahoo Finance
Excel LINEST or Python statsmodels for OLS regression
Plot capability for the security market line
Cost of equity reference data from Damodaran's annual update

review.value: CAPM's central claim is that idiosyncratic risk is uncompensated because it can be diversified away; only systematic risk (beta) is rewarded with higher expected return. This is why concentrating in a single stock is a bad gamble: you bear idiosyncratic risk for which the market does not pay you

A high-volatility stock with low correlation to the market can have a low beta. Beta measures comovement with the market, not standalone risk. A gold mining stock might have high standalone volatility but low beta because gold often moves opposite to equities

CAPM is used billions of times per year in cost-of-equity calculations for DCF valuations, capital budgeting, and regulated utility rate setting. Even people who think CAPM is empirically flawed use it as a starting point because it provides a defensible, widely understood framework

Decades of evidence show small-cap, value, and momentum stocks earn returns CAPM cannot explain. This led to the Fama-French and Carhart multifactor models that we cover next session. CAPM is necessary but not sufficient

class_schedule.topic: Multifactor Models: Fama-French, Carhart, and Beyond
learning_objectives: Explain the empirical motivation for the Fama-French three-factor and five-factor models
Identify the construction of SMB (size), HML (value), RMW (profitability), and CMA (investment) factors
Add a momentum factor (Carhart's UMD/MOM) and explain its empirical and behavioral basis
Use a multifactor regression to decompose a fund's return into factor exposures and alpha
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 10-11 - Arbitrage Pricing Theory and Multifactor Models
Fama & French, 'Common Risk Factors in the Returns on Stocks and Bonds' (Journal of Financial Economics, 1993)
Carhart, 'On Persistence in Mutual Fund Performance' (Journal of Finance, 1997)
Fama & French, 'A Five-Factor Asset Pricing Model' (Journal of Financial Economics, 2015)

lecture_notes.introduction: By the 1980s the empirical case against CAPM as a complete description of expected returns was overwhelming. Banz (1981) documented that small stocks outearned large stocks by far more than CAPM predicted. Rosenberg, Reid, and Lanstein (1985) and DeBondt and Thaler (1985) showed that high book-to-market 'value' stocks outearned low book-to-market 'growth' stocks. Jegadeesh and Titman (1993) showed that stocks that had risen over the past 6-12 months continued to outperform over the next 6-12 months. None of these effects can be explained by beta alone. Eugene Fama and Kenneth French synthesized this evidence in their landmark 1993 paper into a three-factor model: market, size (SMB), and value (HML). Mark Carhart added momentum in 1997. Fama and French extended their model to five factors in 2015 by adding profitability (RMW) and investment (CMA). These models are now the standard in academic finance and the foundation of factor investing, smart beta ETFs, and the entire trillion-dollar industry of style-tilted indexing led by AQR, DFA, and BlackRock. Today we work through the construction of each factor, run a multifactor regression on a real fund, and decompose the fund's return into its factor exposures plus the residual alpha.
key_concepts: Fama-French three-factor model: E[Ri] - Rf = beta_M*(Rm-Rf) + beta_S*SMB + beta_V*HML, where SMB is the size factor and HML is the value factor
SMB construction: Small Minus Big; long the bottom 50% of stocks by market cap, short the top 50%; rebalanced annually
HML construction: High Minus Low; long high book-to-market (value), short low book-to-market (growth); independently sorted on size and book-to-market
Carhart four-factor model: adds UMD (Up Minus Down) or MOM, the momentum factor, long the top 30% past 12-month returns and short the bottom 30%, skipping the most recent month
Fama-French five-factor model: adds RMW (Robust Minus Weak profitability) and CMA (Conservative Minus Aggressive investment); reduces some HML loadings by absorbing them
Factor data source: Kenneth French's data library at Dartmouth provides monthly factor returns going back to 1926 for free; this is the standard data set for academic and practitioner work
Multifactor regression interpretation: the betas measure exposure to each factor (style tilts), and the intercept (alpha) measures risk-adjusted excess return after controlling for factor exposures
Style identification: a fund with high beta_S and high beta_V is a small-cap value fund; a fund with negative beta_S and negative beta_V is a large-cap growth fund

Risk-based versus behavioral interpretations: factors might earn premia because they bear systematic risks (Fama-French view) or because of investor mistakes (DeBondt-Thaler, Lakonishok-Shleifer-Vishny view)

Practitioner extensions: AQR's QMJ (Quality Minus Junk), Asness's defensive equity factors, BAB (Betting Against Beta) by Frazzini and Pedersen; the 'factor zoo' now contains hundreds of proposed factors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Download monthly returns for the Vanguard Small-Cap Value ETF (VBR) and the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF) from 2014-2024. Download Fama-French three-factor and momentum factor data from Kenneth French's website. Run a four-factor regression for each fund. Expect VBR to load heavily on SMB (positive) and HML (positive), with alpha near zero. Expect IWF to load negatively on HML (growth tilt) with negative SMB loading (large cap). Discuss how this regression converts an opaque 'fund return' into an interpretable set of style exposures plus residual skill (alpha).
setup_requirements: Kenneth French's data library from Dartmouth for monthly factor returns
Monthly fund return data for VBR, IWF, and other test funds from Yahoo Finance or Morningstar
Python statsmodels OLS or Excel LINEST for multivariate regression
Reference table for interpreting factor loadings

review.value: A multifactor regression converts a fund's return time series into a set of style exposures: how much exposure to size, value, momentum, profitability, and investment factors. This makes fund comparison apples-to-apples: a 'value fund' that loads heavily on growth should be questioned

Many active managers who reported positive alpha against the S&P 500 actually had zero alpha after controlling for size and value tilts. They were earning factor premia, not skill, and charging active fees for what could be replicated cheaply with a smart-beta ETF

Each factor premium comes with risk: value stocks underperform during long growth-led markets (2010-2020 was brutal for value), momentum suffers during sharp reversals. Factor investing is not arbitrage; it is bearing risks that some investors do not want

Researchers have proposed 300+ factors in the academic literature; most fail out-of-sample replication. Be skeptical of any factor that does not have economic rationale, robust statistical evidence across many time periods, and consistent behavior across geographies

learning_objectives: Price a coupon bond and a zero-coupon bond using present value
Compute current yield, yield-to-maturity, and yield-to-call
Explain the relationship between bond prices and interest rates
Distinguish among Treasury, agency, municipal, investment-grade, and high-yield corporate bonds
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 14 - Bond Prices and Yields
Fabozzi, Bond Markets, Analysis, and Strategies (9th ed.), Ch. 1-3 - Overview, pricing, and yield
Tuckman & Serrat, Fixed Income Securities, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: The U.S. fixed income market is roughly $55 trillion, larger than the U.S. equity market. Treasury securities alone exceed $27 trillion, making them the deepest, most liquid, and most important asset class on the planet. Every interest rate in the economy — mortgages, auto loans, credit cards, corporate borrowing — references the Treasury yield curve. Every pension fund, insurer, and central bank holds bonds as a core portion of its portfolio. Yet bonds are systematically misunderstood by investors who think of them as 'safe.' Bonds carry interest rate risk (price falls when yields rise), credit risk (issuer may default), inflation risk (fixed nominal coupons lose real value), and liquidity risk (some bonds rarely trade). Today we begin a three-session sequence on fixed income with the foundational mechanics: how to price a bond from its cash flows, how to compute the various yield measures investors quote, and how to distinguish the major sectors of the bond market. By the end of class you will be able to take a bond's coupon, maturity, and price and compute its yield-to-maturity by hand or by Excel YIELD function, and you will understand exactly why a 30-year Treasury fell 30%+ in price during the 2022 rate-rise cycle.
key_concepts: Bond cash flows: a coupon bond pays a periodic coupon (typically semiannual in the U.S.) and the face value at maturity; a zero-coupon bond pays only the face value at maturity
Bond pricing formula: P = sum of C/(1+y)^t for t=1 to N, plus F/(1+y)^N, where C is the periodic coupon, F is face value, y is the periodic discount rate, and N is the number of periods
Zero-coupon pricing: P = F/(1+y)^N; a 10-year zero with face $1000 and yield 5% is worth 1000/(1.05)^10 = $613.91
Current yield: annual coupon / current price; a quick approximation that ignores capital gains/losses to maturity
Yield-to-maturity (YTM): the single discount rate that makes the present value of all cash flows equal to the price; the IRR of the bond's cash flows assuming hold to maturity
Yield-to-call (YTC): for callable bonds, the YTM computed assuming the bond is called at the earliest call date and call price; investors price callable bonds at the lower of YTM and YTC
Inverse price-yield relationship: when yields rise, prices fall; when yields fall, prices rise. The relationship is convex (curved), not linear
Premium versus discount: a bond trading above face is at a 'premium' (coupon > yield); below face is at a 'discount' (coupon < yield); at face is 'at par' (coupon = yield)

Sector taxonomy: Treasuries (default-risk-free), agencies (Fannie/Freddie/Ginnie), municipals (state/local, often tax-exempt), investment-grade corporates (BBB-/Baa3 and above), high-yield corporates (below BBB-)
Credit ratings: S&P, Moody's, Fitch rate creditworthiness from AAA (highest) down through D (default); rating downgrades can move prices sharply, especially across the IG/HY threshold
lecture_notes.demonstration: Price a 5-year, 4% annual coupon Treasury bond when the yield is 3% (premium, near $1045.80), 4% (par, $1000), and 5% (discount, near $956.71). Compute the current yield in each case. Then take a real callable corporate bond (e.g., a 30-year Apple bond callable at year 5) and compute YTM versus YTC. Show how the lower of the two governs the bond's effective yield. Plot the price-yield relationship for a 10-year and a 30-year zero-coupon bond on the same chart and observe that the 30-year is far more sensitive to yield changes — the foundation of duration.
setup_requirements: Excel PRICE, YIELD, YIELDMAT functions for bond pricing
Sample bond list: Treasury notes, an investment-grade corporate, a high-yield corporate, a callable bond
Current Treasury yield curve from Treasury.gov
Credit rating reference table

review.value: When market interest rates rise, the present value of fixed future cash flows falls; bond prices drop. The 2022 rate-rise cycle pushed the 10-year Treasury yield from 1.5% to 4.2% and dropped the 30-year zero by roughly 35% — a stock-market-like loss in what investors thought of as the 'safe' asset

review.key: YTM Assumes Hold to Maturity and Reinvestment
review.value: YTM is the IRR of the bond cash flows. It assumes you hold to maturity and reinvest each coupon at the same YTM. Both assumptions can fail: you might sell early, and reinvestment rates change with the yield curve. Realized yield can differ substantially from quoted YTM

If rates fall, the issuer calls the bond and you lose the upside; if rates rise, you keep a below-market bond. Callable bonds therefore must offer higher yields than otherwise identical non-callable bonds to compensate for this short-call position

Rating agencies often downgrade after the market has already moved. Watch credit spreads (corporate yield minus Treasury yield) directly; a widening spread is the market saying credit risk is rising before any rating action

class_schedule.topic: Duration, Convexity, and Interest Rate Risk
learning_objectives: Compute Macaulay duration and modified duration for a coupon bond
Use modified duration to estimate the price change for a small yield change
Compute convexity and apply the duration-plus-convexity adjustment for larger yield changes
Apply duration matching to immunize a portfolio against interest rate risk
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 16 - Managing Bond Portfolios
Fabozzi, Bond Markets, Analysis, and Strategies (9th ed.), Ch. 4-5 - Duration and Convexity
Tuckman & Serrat, Fixed Income Securities, Ch. 4-5

lecture_notes.introduction: If you tell me you own a 10-year Treasury, I do not know how much money you will lose if rates rise 100 basis points. If you tell me your portfolio has modified duration of 6.5 and rates rise 100 bps, I can immediately tell you that you will lose approximately 6.5%. Duration is the single most important number in fixed income because it converts the abstract idea of 'interest rate sensitivity' into a quantitative price-change estimate that any portfolio manager can act on. Macaulay duration was developed in 1938 by Frederick Macaulay, who was looking for a single number that captured a bond's effective maturity by weighting each cash flow by its present value. Modified duration adapted Macaulay's measure into the linear price-change predictor that practitioners use every day. Convexity captures the second-order effect: bonds with higher convexity gain more when yields fall and lose less when yields rise than duration alone would predict, which is why convexity is a desirable property even though it is more expensive to acquire. Today we work through both measures, see how they combine into the duration-plus-convexity price approximation, and apply them to immunize a pension fund's liabilities against parallel shifts in the yield curve. This is the daily work of every fixed income manager at PIMCO, BlackRock, and Vanguard.
key_concepts: Macaulay duration: weighted average time to receive cash flows, where weights are present value fractions of total bond price
Modified duration: D_mod = D_Mac / (1 + y/k), where y is YTM and k is coupon periods per year; modified duration is the percentage price change per unit yield change
Price-change formula: dP/P approximately equals -D_mod * dy; a bond with modified duration 7 loses approximately 7% in price when yield rises 1% (100 basis points)
Duration drivers: longer maturity increases duration; lower coupon increases duration (more weight on terminal payment); lower yield increases duration
Zero-coupon bond duration: equals time-to-maturity; a 10-year zero has Macaulay duration of exactly 10 years
Portfolio duration: weighted average of individual bond durations using market value weights; this additivity makes duration the key tool for portfolio-level interest rate risk management
Convexity: the second derivative of price with respect to yield, normalized by price; higher convexity means the price-yield curve is more curved
Duration-plus-convexity approximation: dP/P approximately equals -D_mod * dy + 0.5 * convexity * dy^2; the convexity term captures the asymmetry that duration alone misses

Immunization: matching the duration of assets to the duration of liabilities so that small parallel yield-curve shifts have offsetting effects on both sides of the balance sheet; foundational technique in pension and insurance asset-liability management

Effective duration for callable bonds: standard duration formulas fail for bonds with embedded options because cash flows depend on yields; effective duration uses parallel yield-curve shifts and revaluation through an option-adjusted model
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute Macaulay duration for a 5-year 4% annual coupon bond yielding 4% by hand: cash flows {40, 40, 40, 40, 1040}, present values, weights, and weighted average time. Result: about 4.63 years. Compute modified duration: 4.63/1.04 = 4.45. Predict the price change for a 50 bps yield rise: -4.45 * 0.005 = -2.225%. Then compute convexity (~24.4) and apply the full approximation: -4.45*0.005 + 0.5*24.4*(0.005)^2 = -2.195%. Compare to the exact reprice (-2.197%) to show the convexity correction works.
setup_requirements: Excel template with Macaulay duration, modified duration, and convexity formulas
Sample bond cash flow worksheet for calculations by hand
Yield curve data for portfolio immunization exercise
Reference: Bloomberg YA function for duration and convexity outputs

review.value: Modified duration converts the abstract 'how much rate risk' question into a concrete percentage price change for a parallel yield shift. Every bond manager monitors portfolio duration daily and adjusts to match a target benchmark

Higher-convexity bonds gain more on rate drops and lose less on rate rises than duration alone predicts. This is why mortgage-backed securities (which have negative convexity due to prepayment) trade at higher yields than equivalent-duration Treasuries
Duration matching protects against small parallel yield shifts only. Twists in the yield curve and large shifts require more sophisticated techniques like key-rate duration or full scenario analysis

A zero-coupon bond's Macaulay duration equals its maturity exactly because there is only one cash flow. This is why STRIPS are popular for duration extension: you can buy a single 30-year cash flow with maximum duration

class_schedule.topic: Equity Valuation: DCF, Dividend Discount Models, and Multiples
learning_objectives: Apply the dividend discount model in zero-growth, constant-growth (Gordon), and multi-stage forms
Build a free cash flow to equity (FCFE) and free cash flow to firm (FCFF) DCF model
Use multiples (P/E, EV/EBITDA, P/B, EV/Sales) for relative valuation and identify their pitfalls
Reconcile DCF and multiples valuations and explain when each is more appropriate
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 18 - Equity Valuation Models
Damodaran, Investment Valuation (3rd ed.), Ch. 13-15 - Equity DCF and DDM, Ch. 17-19 Multiples
Graham, The Intelligent Investor (rev. ed.), Ch. 11-14 - Valuation discipline and margin of safety

lecture_notes.introduction: Aswath Damodaran, the 'dean of valuation' at NYU Stern, opens his investment valuation textbook with a sentence that should be tattooed on every analyst's forearm: 'The value of an asset is the present value of its expected cash flows.' Everything else in equity valuation — multiples, residual income models, dividend discount models, free cash flow models — is a different way of approximating that single proposition under different assumptions and data constraints. Today's session is the most practically useful in the course because it teaches you how to convert publicly available financial statements into an estimate of intrinsic value that you can compare to a market price. We will work through three valuation approaches: the dividend discount model (which works well for stable dividend-paying companies like utilities and consumer staples), the discounted cash flow model (which works for any company that generates cash and is the standard tool of equity research analysts), and relative valuation via multiples (which works for everything but requires careful peer selection and multiple choice). Each approach has its place, and skilled analysts triangulate across all three. Warren Buffett's lifetime track record at Berkshire Hathaway is a testament to the power of patient intrinsic-value investing: he buys when DCF gives a price meaningfully above the market quote (margin of safety) and holds until the gap closes.
key_concepts: Dividend discount model (DDM): P0 = sum of D_t / (1+r)^t, the present value of all future dividends discounted at the cost of equity
Gordon growth model: P0 = D1 / (r - g), valid when dividends grow at a constant rate g forever and r > g; the simplest closed-form valuation
Two-stage DDM: high-growth phase followed by stable growth; P0 = sum of high-growth dividend PVs plus terminal value computed via Gordon growth
Free cash flow to equity (FCFE): cash flow available to equity holders after debt service and reinvestment; FCFE = Net Income + Depreciation - CapEx - Change in WC - Net Debt Repayment
Free cash flow to firm (FCFF): cash flow before debt payments; FCFF = EBIT*(1-t) + Depreciation - CapEx - Change in WC; discounted at WACC to get enterprise value
Discount rate selection: cost of equity (CAPM) for FCFE and DDM; weighted average cost of capital (WACC) for FCFF
Terminal value: typically Gordon growth applied to year-N+1 cash flow, or exit multiple (e.g., 10x EBITDA at year 10); often the largest single component of total value
P/E ratio: price per share / earnings per share; trailing P/E uses last 12 months earnings, forward P/E uses next 12 months estimates
EV/EBITDA: enterprise value (market cap + debt - cash) divided by EBITDA; capital-structure-neutral, popular for cross-company comparison
Price-to-book (P/B): price / book value of equity; classic value metric; meaningful for asset-heavy businesses (banks, utilities) but breaks for asset-light tech firms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a Gordon growth valuation for Coca-Cola (KO): D1 = $1.94, r (cost of equity from CAPM with beta 0.6) near 7.3%, g (long-term sustainable growth) near 4%; P0 = 1.94 / (0.073 - 0.04) = $58.79. Compare to market price. Then build a two-stage FCFF DCF for Microsoft using next-five-year FCFF projections and a Gordon terminal value with WACC near 9% and terminal g = 3.5%. Finally, compute trailing P/E, forward P/E, and EV/EBITDA for Microsoft, Apple, and Alphabet and discuss why their multiples differ.
setup_requirements: Financial statements for KO, MSFT, AAPL, GOOGL from SEC EDGAR or Yahoo Finance
Excel template with DDM, FCFE, and FCFF tabs
Damodaran's industry beta and WACC tables for benchmarking
Analyst consensus growth forecasts from public sources

review.value: A DCF valuation is hypersensitive to terminal growth and discount rate. For Microsoft, changing terminal g from 3% to 4% can move intrinsic value by 20%+. Always present DCF as a range (sensitivity table) not a point estimate, and disclose your key assumptions

Comparing Tesla's P/E to Ford's P/E is meaningless; they have different growth, margins, and risk. Multiples comparison only works when peers are genuinely similar in business model, growth, and capital intensity. Multiples are quick but easy to misuse

The Gordon model requires r > g; if you assume g > r, the model produces nonsense (negative or infinite price). Long-term g must be at most the long-run economy growth rate because no firm can grow faster than the economy forever

Benjamin Graham's discipline: buy only when intrinsic value is meaningfully above market price (e.g., 30% margin). DCF is imprecise; the margin of safety protects you from being wrong. Buffett: 'It is far better to buy a wonderful company at a fair price than a fair company at a wonderful price'

class_schedule.topic: Behavioral Finance: Biases and Market Anomalies
learning_objectives: Identify the major cognitive biases documented by Kahneman and Tversky relevant to investing
Distinguish between the rational expectations / efficient markets paradigm and the behavioral paradigm
Connect specific biases to specific market anomalies (momentum, post-earnings drift, bubble formation)
Apply behavioral insights to portfolio design, including default options and rebalancing rules
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 12 - Behavioral Finance and Technical Analysis
Kahneman, Thinking, Fast and Slow, Part 4 - Choices
Thaler, Misbehaving, Ch. 1-3 and the saving chapter
Shiller, Irrational Exuberance, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Eugene Fama and Richard Thaler, both at the University of Chicago, won Nobel Prizes for fundamentally opposing views of how markets work. Fama (1970, 2013 Nobel) argued that markets are efficient: prices reflect all available information, and investors are rational. Thaler (2017 Nobel) argued the opposite: investors are systematically biased, and these biases produce predictable patterns in prices. Daniel Kahneman, a psychologist at Princeton who won the 2002 Nobel in economics for work he did with Amos Tversky, supplied the experimental evidence that humans deviate from rational choice in specific, replicable ways. Loss aversion: losses hurt about twice as much as equivalent gains feel good. Anchoring: judgments are pulled toward irrelevant starting numbers. Availability: we overweight events we remember vividly. Overconfidence: we systematically overestimate our skill, especially men trading stocks. Today's session presents the catalog of biases, connects them to documented market anomalies, and asks the practical question: if you know investors are biased, can you build a portfolio that avoids the biases — or better, exploits them? The answer is yes, and it is the foundation of factor investing, target-date funds, automatic rebalancing, and the behavioral nudges Thaler and Sunstein wrote about in 'Nudge.' This is the most important session for any investor who wants to manage their own money: most of the damage retail investors do to themselves is psychological, not informational.
key_concepts: Loss aversion (Kahneman-Tversky): the pain of a loss is roughly 2x the pleasure of an equivalent gain; explains the disposition effect (selling winners too soon, holding losers too long)

Anchoring: estimates are pulled toward an arbitrary reference point; investors anchor to purchase price ('I will sell when I get back to even') even though purchase price is irrelevant to current value
Availability heuristic: events that come to mind easily seem more probable; recent crashes feel more likely than they are, leading to over-pricing of tail risk after a crisis
Overconfidence: investors overestimate their skill; Barber and Odean's studies of 65,000 brokerage accounts found men traded 45% more than women and earned 1.4% lower net returns due to trading costs
Herding: investors follow the crowd; produces bubbles when sentiment is positive and crashes when sentiment turns; documented in mutual fund flows (chase past winners, dump past losers)

Mental accounting: money is treated differently based on which 'bucket' it belongs to; investors separate 'safe money' (bonds) from 'play money' (individual stocks) instead of optimizing the whole portfolio
Confirmation bias: investors seek evidence that confirms their existing views and ignore contradictory information; produces stale beliefs and slow updating
Hindsight bias: after an event, investors remember themselves as having predicted it ('I knew the market would crash'); leads to overconfidence in future predictions
Prospect theory value function: concave for gains, convex for losses, with a kink at the reference point; the formal model of loss aversion that explains many real-world choices
Behavioral anomalies: post-earnings announcement drift, momentum (winners keep winning), value premium (cheap stocks beat expensive), all attributed to biased investor behavior
lecture_notes.demonstration: Run a classroom experiment: ask students to choose between (a) sure $50 versus 50/50 shot at $100. Most pick (a). Then ask: (a) sure loss of $50 versus 50/50 shot at -$100. Most pick (b). This is loss aversion in action. Then walk through Brad Barber and Terrance Odean's data showing the typical retail investor underperforms the market by 1.5% per year due to overtrading, and Shiller's CAPE chart showing that high CAPE periods (like 2000 and 2021) have systematically led to lower forward 10-year returns. Discuss the GameStop short squeeze of January 2021 as a real-time case study in herding, social proof, and Availability cascades.
setup_requirements: Slides with Kahneman's loss aversion experiments and prospect theory value function
Barber-Odean retail investor performance data
Shiller CAPE ratio chart from his Yale website
GameStop price chart from January 2021

review.value: These biases are not weaknesses of dumb investors; they are documented in PhD economists, hedge fund managers, and Nobel laureates. The first step in managing them is acknowledging that you have them too

Investors sell winning positions to 'lock in gains' and hold losing positions to 'avoid realizing losses.' Tax rules actually favor the opposite (sell losers for tax loss harvesting). The disposition effect is a behavioral cost that disciplined investors avoid

Thaler and Benartzi's 'Save More Tomorrow' showed that opt-out 401(k) enrollment dramatically increases savings rates compared to opt-in. Default options for asset allocation (target-date funds) similarly produce better outcomes than letting investors choose. Architecture beats willpower

You cannot rid yourself of biases by trying harder. You need a process: written investment policy, automatic rebalancing, dollar-cost averaging, no trading after market hours, no trading after big losses. The process is the antidote to the bias

class_schedule.topic: Active vs Passive Management and the Efficient Market Debate
learning_objectives: State the three forms of the efficient market hypothesis (weak, semi-strong, strong) and the evidence for each
Summarize the SPIVA scorecard evidence on active manager performance versus passive benchmarks
Explain the Grossman-Stiglitz paradox and the role of arbitrageurs in keeping markets efficient
Analyze the rise of index funds from Vanguard's founding (1975) to the present
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 11 - The Efficient Market Hypothesis
Malkiel, A Random Walk Down Wall Street (13th ed.), Ch. 7-9
Bogle, The Little Book of Common Sense Investing - the index fund case
S&P SPIVA U.S. Scorecard, most recent year-end report

lecture_notes.introduction: On December 31, 1975, John C. Bogle launched the First Index Investment Trust at Vanguard, which became the Vanguard 500 Index Fund. It was widely mocked as 'Bogle's folly' — why would anyone buy a fund that promised only to match the market when active managers promised to beat it? Forty-nine years later, that fund and its successor are at the center of an industry that has redirected more than $10 trillion from active management to passive index strategies, including the largest pension funds, endowments, and sovereign wealth funds in the world. The question 'should I be active or passive' is the most consequential one a retail investor or an institutional allocator can ask, and the evidence has become overwhelming: net of fees, the median active manager underperforms a low-cost index fund. The S&P SPIVA scorecards have documented this every year for two decades. The 2023 SPIVA report shows 60% of large-cap U.S. equity funds underperformed the S&P 500 over one year, 87% over five years, 93% over ten years, and 96% over twenty years. Mid-cap and small-cap active managers do no better despite the common claim that 'small caps are inefficient.' Yet the case is not closed: a small minority of managers do outperform persistently (Renaissance Technologies' Medallion fund, AQR, some private equity), and the question of whether outperformance is skill or luck is genuinely hard. Today we examine the efficient market hypothesis, the empirical record, and the practical implications for portfolio construction.
key_concepts: Weak-form EMH: prices reflect all past price information; technical analysis cannot consistently produce excess returns
Semi-strong-form EMH: prices reflect all publicly available information; fundamental analysis cannot consistently produce excess returns
Strong-form EMH: prices reflect all information including private/insider information; even insider trading cannot produce excess returns (clearly false in practice)
Random walk hypothesis: stock prices follow a random walk so successive price changes are statistically independent; the precursor to weak-form EMH

Grossman-Stiglitz paradox: if markets were perfectly efficient, no one would be paid to gather information, so prices would not reflect information; thus markets must be slightly inefficient to compensate active researchers

SPIVA evidence: S&P's annual scorecards show ~85% of active U.S. equity funds underperform their benchmarks over 10-year periods after fees; persistence studies show winners do not consistently repeat
Survivorship bias: poorly performing funds are merged or closed; index of surviving funds overstates the performance of the original universe; SPIVA accounts for this
Cost matters most: average active fund expense ratio near 1%, average index fund near 0.05%; over 30 years, this difference compounds to roughly 25% of terminal wealth
Index fund types: market-cap weighted (S&P 500, total market), equal weighted, factor-tilted (smart beta), thematic (clean energy, AI); the term 'passive' has blurred

ETF vs mutual fund: ETFs trade intraday on exchanges, mutual funds trade once daily at NAV; ETFs have tax advantages from in-kind redemption; expense ratios for index ETFs and mutual funds have converged near zero
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the latest SPIVA U.S. Scorecard table by table. Show that 87.4% of large-cap funds underperformed over 5 years, 93.4% over 10 years, 96.5% over 20 years. Then compute the wealth difference: $10,000 invested in 1976 in the Vanguard 500 Index Fund vs the average actively managed mutual fund, accounting for fees and survivorship. Vanguard 500 ends near $1.2M; the average active fund ends near $700-800K. Discuss the few persistent outperformers: Buffett at Berkshire, Renaissance Medallion (closed to outsiders), David Tepper at Appaloosa, and the question of whether their success is replicable.
setup_requirements: Most recent SPIVA U.S. Scorecard PDF from S&P Dow Jones Indices
Vanguard 500 Index Fund historical returns from Vanguard.com
Bogle's Little Book of Common Sense Investing as reference
Morningstar fund cost data for cost-impact calculations

review.value: The single most reliable predictor of fund underperformance is its expense ratio. Over 30 years, a 1% fee differential compounds to roughly 25% less terminal wealth. Active managers must overcome this hurdle every year, and most cannot

If outperformance were skill, we would expect winners to keep winning. SPIVA persistence studies show that top-quartile funds in one period are no more likely than bottom-quartile funds to be top-quartile in the next period. This is what we expect if performance is mostly luck

Markets are efficient enough that the typical investor cannot reliably exploit inefficiencies after costs, but inefficient enough that a small number of sophisticated managers can extract excess returns through research, technology, and access. The retail investor's best response is to be passive

Active management may still add value in less efficient corners: distressed debt, frontier markets, micro-cap, private equity, real estate. The case against active is strongest for large-cap U.S. equity, where information is most widely shared and competition is fiercest

learning_objectives: Compute Sharpe ratio, Treynor ratio, Jensen's alpha, information ratio, and M-squared
Explain when each performance measure is appropriate
Conduct a simple factor-based performance attribution
Critique the misuse of performance statistics in fund marketing
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 24 - Portfolio Performance Evaluation
Sharpe, 'Mutual Fund Performance' (Journal of Business, 1966) - the original Sharpe ratio paper
Grinold & Kahn, Active Portfolio Management, Ch. 4-5

lecture_notes.introduction: A fund reports a 12% return last year. Is that good? You have no idea. It depends on the volatility (was the fund taking 30% standalone risk to generate that 12%?), the beta (was the fund just amplifying market movements?), the benchmark (was the asset class up 15% on average?), the fees (was the gross return 13% with a 1% management fee?), and the time period (was last year cherry-picked from a worse 10-year record?). Performance measurement converts raw returns into risk-adjusted, benchmark-relative numbers that you can actually compare across funds and across time. This is the daily work of every consultant who advises pension funds, every investment committee that hires and fires managers, and every sophisticated allocator who decides where to put their next dollar. Today we work through the four classic risk-adjusted performance measures: Sharpe (return per unit of total risk), Treynor (return per unit of beta), Jensen's alpha (excess return relative to CAPM), and information ratio (active return per unit of active risk). We also cover M-squared, a variant of Sharpe that converts to percentage points of return at a benchmark's volatility, and we walk through the difference between time-weighted and money-weighted returns — a distinction that has caused real lawsuits. By the end of class you will be able to evaluate any fund's track record critically and identify the marketing tricks that mask poor performance.
key_concepts: Sharpe ratio: (E[Rp] - Rf) / sigma_p, excess return per unit of total risk; the most widely used measure of risk-adjusted return; appropriate when the portfolio represents the entire risky portion of the investor's wealth
Treynor ratio: (E[Rp] - Rf) / beta_p, excess return per unit of systematic risk; appropriate when the portfolio is one component of a larger diversified portfolio (so only beta matters)
Jensen's alpha: alpha = E[Rp] - [Rf + beta_p * (E[Rm] - Rf)], the intercept from a CAPM regression; positive alpha means excess return above CAPM-predicted return
Information ratio: (E[Rp] - E[Rb]) / sigma(Rp - Rb), active return divided by tracking error; the standard measure of active manager skill
M-squared (Modigliani-Modigliani): leverages or de-leverages the portfolio to match the market's volatility, then compares to the market return; expressed in percentage points
Time-weighted return (TWR): geometric chain-link of period returns; isolates portfolio manager performance from cash flow timing; required by GIPS standards
Money-weighted return (MWR / IRR): the IRR of all cash flows in and out of the portfolio; reflects what the investor actually earned but is contaminated by deposit/withdrawal timing

Performance attribution: decomposes the return difference between portfolio and benchmark into asset allocation effect, security selection effect, and interaction effect (Brinson-Hood-Beebower 1986 framework)
Style drift: when a manager's actual factor exposures deviate from their stated style; revealed by rolling factor regressions; a red flag for risk control
Benchmark selection bias: managers can game any single performance measure by choosing the right benchmark; the only defense is to use multiple measures and an independent benchmark mandate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Two hypothetical funds. Fund A: 12% return, 14% volatility, beta 0.9, tracking error 4%. Fund B: 11% return, 10% volatility, beta 0.7, tracking error 3%. Risk-free rate 3%, market 10%, market vol 15%. Compute Sharpe ratios: A = (12-3)/14 = 0.643, B = (11-3)/10 = 0.800. Treynor: A = 9/0.9 = 10.0, B = 8/0.7 = 11.4. Jensen's alpha: A = 12 - (3 + 0.9*7) = 2.7%, B = 11 - (3 + 0.7*7) = 3.1%. Information ratio against market: A = (12-10)/4 = 0.50, B = (11-10)/3 = 0.33. Discuss: Fund B looks better on all standalone measures but Fund A has a higher information ratio relative to the market.
setup_requirements: Excel template with Sharpe, Treynor, Jensen, IR formulas
Sample monthly return data for two real funds and the S&P 500
GIPS standard handbook reference
Brinson-Hood-Beebower attribution worksheet

review.value: Sharpe ratio is correct when the portfolio is your only risky investment; Treynor ratio is correct when it is one of many holdings (so only beta risk is added). Most retail investors are in the Sharpe situation, while institutional allocators evaluate sleeves with Treynor

TWR isolates manager skill; MWR reflects investor experience. A fund can have a great TWR but a poor MWR if investors panicked and sold at the bottom — common with volatile funds. ARK Innovation ETF is a famous recent example

Genuine alpha (Jensen's) requires beating the CAPM-predicted return after fees. Most funds with reported positive alpha against the S&P 500 lose their alpha after controlling for size and value tilts in a multifactor regression. True multi-factor alpha is rare

review.key: Information Ratio Is the Pro Measure
review.value: Professional fund evaluation focuses on information ratio: how much active return per unit of active risk (tracking error). An IR of 0.5 is good; 1.0 is excellent and rare; greater than 1.0 over 10+ years suggests genuine skill

learning_objectives: Distinguish strategic, tactical, and dynamic asset allocation
Construct a strategic asset allocation for a representative individual investor
Apply rebalancing rules (calendar, threshold, hybrid) and analyze their impact
Discuss life-cycle / target-date allocation and the equity glide path
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 28 - Investment Policy and the Framework of the CFA Institute
Brinson, Hood & Beebower, 'Determinants of Portfolio Performance' (Financial Analysts Journal, 1986)
Bernstein, The Intelligent Asset Allocator, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Asset allocation is the most consequential decision in portfolio management. Brinson, Hood, and Beebower's 1986 study of 91 large pension funds found that asset allocation policy explained 93.6% of the variability of total return across funds. Security selection and market timing combined explained the rest. Thirty-eight years and many replication studies later, the conclusion is durable: how you split your money between stocks, bonds, and other asset classes matters far more than which specific stocks or bonds you pick. This is the core insight that target-date retirement funds are built on, and target-date funds now manage more than $3 trillion in U.S. defined-contribution plans. Today we work through the practical mechanics of building a portfolio for a real investor: defining objectives and constraints (return target, risk tolerance, time horizon, taxes, liquidity, regulatory), translating those into a strategic asset allocation (the long-term policy mix), allowing tactical deviations (short-term tilts away from policy), and rebalancing back to policy. We will also cover the life-cycle / glide-path approach where equity allocation decreases as the investor ages — a structure that has become the default for retirement plans. By the end of class you will be able to write an investment policy statement and propose a strategic allocation for a 35-year-old saving for retirement, a 65-year-old recently retired, and a university endowment with a perpetual time horizon.
key_concepts: Strategic asset allocation (SAA): the long-term policy mix that an investor expects to hold on average over a multi-year horizon; based on expected returns, risks, and correlations across asset classes
Tactical asset allocation (TAA): short-term deviations from SAA based on market views; e.g., overweight equities when valuations are cheap, underweight when expensive
Dynamic asset allocation: rule-based rebalancing tied to changing market conditions, often risk-targeted (volatility targeting, risk parity)

Rebalancing methods: (1) calendar rebalancing — quarterly or annual; (2) threshold rebalancing — rebalance when allocation drifts more than X% from target; (3) hybrid: check on calendar, rebalance only if threshold breached
Rebalancing benefit: forces a 'sell high, buy low' discipline that increases long-run returns relative to a buy-and-hold portfolio (typically by 20-50 bps annually) and controls risk drift

Investment policy statement (IPS): written document defining objectives, constraints, asset allocation, rebalancing rules, and benchmarks; required for institutional portfolios and recommended for individuals
Life-cycle investing: equity allocation decreases as investor approaches retirement; typical target-date fund glide path is 90% equities at age 25 declining to 30% equities at age 65
Liability-driven investing (LDI): pension funds and insurers match asset duration and cash flows to liabilities rather than maximizing return; since 2008 has dominated DB pension management
Risk parity: weight assets so each contributes equal risk to the portfolio; popularized by Bridgewater All Weather; tends to overweight bonds and use leverage
Endowment model: heavy alternative allocations (private equity, real assets, hedge funds), long-horizon focus, illiquidity premium harvest; pioneered by David Swensen at Yale
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a strategic asset allocation for a 35-year-old saving for retirement: time horizon ~30 years, high risk tolerance, taxable account. Recommend 80% global equities (50% U.S., 30% international), 15% bonds (intermediate Treasuries and corporate IG), 5% real assets (REITs). Then build for a 65-year-old retiree: 50% equities, 40% bonds, 10% cash equivalents. Then for a university endowment with perpetual horizon and 5% spending rule: 35% public equity, 10% bonds, 20% private equity, 15% hedge funds, 15% real assets, 5% cash. Discuss the tradeoffs in each. Finally, simulate annual rebalancing for the 35-year-old over 2008-2024 and show the discipline forced selling stocks in 2021 and buying in 2008 and 2022.
setup_requirements: CFA Institute IPS template
Long-run capital market expectations from major asset managers (BlackRock, JPM, Vanguard publish annually)
Excel rebalancing simulator with monthly returns
Yale endowment annual report for endowment model reference

review.value: Brinson, Hood, and Beebower (1986) showed asset allocation explains 90%+ of return variability across pension funds. Spend the most time on the allocation decision; security selection is a much smaller lever

Rebalancing forces selling what has gone up and buying what has gone down — exactly what investors find emotionally hardest. Mechanical rules (annual or threshold) bypass the emotion and capture the rebalancing premium of 20-50 bps annually

Life-cycle equity glide paths assume human capital (future earnings) is bond-like, so investors should hold more financial equities when young and shift to bonds as human capital depletes. The practical effect is reasonable: younger investors can ride out volatility, retirees cannot

A written investment policy statement is the antidote to behavioral biases. When markets crash and you are tempted to sell, the IPS reminds you of your time horizon, risk tolerance, and policy allocation. No IPS, no discipline

class_schedule.topic: Alternative Investments: Hedge Funds, Private Equity, Real Assets
learning_objectives: Identify the major hedge fund strategies (long/short equity, global macro, event-driven, relative value, managed futures)
Explain private equity structures (LBO, growth equity, venture capital) and the J-curve
Distinguish among real assets: real estate, infrastructure, commodities, natural resources
Critically evaluate alternative investment performance net of fees and the illiquidity premium
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 26 - Hedge Funds
Lhabitant, Hedge Funds: Quantitative Insights, Ch. 1-5
Lerner, Leamon & Hardymon, Venture Capital, Private Equity, and the Financing of Entrepreneurship (2nd ed.), Ch. 1-3
Swensen, Pioneering Portfolio Management, Ch. 7-9

lecture_notes.introduction: The 'alternatives' label covers everything that does not fit neatly into long-only public stocks and bonds: hedge funds, private equity, venture capital, real estate, infrastructure, commodities, hedged credit, managed futures. Together they manage roughly $13 trillion globally and have become a core allocation for pension funds, endowments, sovereign wealth funds, and high-net-worth investors. David Swensen's work at the Yale endowment from 1985 until his death in 2021 is the canonical example: by allocating heavily to alternatives — private equity, hedge funds, real assets — Yale grew from $1B to over $40B and inspired the 'endowment model' that hundreds of institutions now follow. Yet the case for alternatives is more contested than the marketing brochures suggest. Hedge fund returns have lagged simple stock-bond portfolios since 2009. Private equity returns shrink to roughly market-equivalent levels after adjusting for leverage and the illiquidity of the asset class. Venture capital returns are highly skewed: a small number of top-quartile funds generate huge returns and the rest lose money on a risk-adjusted basis. The high fees ('2 and 20' is still common) eat much of any outperformance. Today we work through the menu of alternatives, the structures they use, the J-curve dynamics of private capital, and the honest performance evidence. By the end of class you will be able to evaluate when an alternative allocation is justified for a portfolio and when it is just expensive beta in disguise.
key_concepts: Hedge fund strategies: long/short equity, global macro, event-driven (M&A arbitrage, distressed debt), relative value (statistical arbitrage, fixed income arb), managed futures / CTA (trend-following)

Hedge fund structure: limited partnership; GP manages, LPs invest; typical fees '2 and 20' (2% management, 20% performance above hurdle); high water mark protects investors from paying performance fees on recouped losses

Private equity types: leveraged buyout (LBO) — acquire mature companies with debt; growth equity — minority investments in scaling companies; venture capital — seed/Series A-D investments in startups; mezzanine and special situations
LBO mechanics: PE firm buys a target with 60-80% debt and 20-40% equity, improves operations or financial engineering, exits in 4-7 years via IPO, secondary sale, or strategic acquisition
J-curve: private equity returns are negative in early years (fees and write-offs of failing investments) and turn positive in later years (winners are realized); the cumulative IRR forms a J-shape
Vintage year effect: PE returns vary dramatically by vintage year; funds raised at market peaks (2007, 2021) historically underperform those raised in trough years
Real estate: direct ownership, REITs (publicly traded), private real estate funds; income-producing or development; subject to interest rate risk, location risk, and operational risk
Infrastructure: toll roads, airports, power generation, fiber networks; long-duration cash flows, often inflation-linked; favored by pension funds for liability matching

Commodities and managed futures: futures contracts on energy, metals, grains; benefits from 'roll yield' in backwardation, suffers in contango; inflation hedge with low correlation to equities historically

Net-of-fee performance reality: hedge fund index (HFRI) has trailed a 60/40 stock-bond portfolio since 2009 by hundreds of basis points annually after fees; PE outperformance shrinks to zero after adjusting for leverage and illiquidity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the Long-Term Capital Management collapse in 1998: brilliant founders (including Nobel laureates Myron Scholes and Robert Merton), highly leveraged relative-value strategies, fund grew to $4.7B equity controlling $129B in assets and $1.25T notional in derivatives. Russia defaulted in August 1998 and correlations across LTCM's 'unrelated' positions all spiked to 1, producing $4.6B in losses in four months. The Federal Reserve organized a $3.6B bailout. Then walk through Renaissance Medallion (closed to outside investors since 2005, audited net returns near 40% annually) and Archegos Capital's 2021 implosion where $20B in family office capital became $0 in four days due to concentrated leveraged equity swaps.
setup_requirements: Roger Lowenstein, When Genius Failed, for the LTCM story
Greg Zuckerman, The Man Who Solved the Market, for the Renaissance story
Phalippou's research papers on PE net-of-fee returns
Yale endowment annual report for the endowment model

review.value: 'Hedge funds' is not an asset class — it is a fee structure. The strategies range from market-neutral statistical arbitrage to leveraged global macro bets. Lump-summing 'hedge funds' for asset allocation is a category error; you need to understand the specific strategy and its risk profile

2 and 20 plus management fees plus performance fees plus fund-of-fund fees can total 5-7% annually in the worst cases. Even outstanding gross returns often shrink to mediocre net returns after the fee waterfall

Locking up capital in PE for 10+ years requires compensation. The standard estimate is 3-5% annually as the illiquidity premium. PE returns net of this premium are roughly market-equivalent; the case for PE is therefore the illiquidity premium plus genuine GP value-add

Private capital investors must be psychologically prepared for negative returns in the early years of a fund. Selling in the J-curve trough (as happens in market crises with secondary discounts of 30-50%) destroys returns. The endowment model works only with genuine perpetual time horizon

learning_objectives: Apply tax-aware investing techniques: asset location, tax-loss harvesting, low-turnover indexing
Distinguish among ESG screening, integration, thematic, and impact investing
Evaluate the empirical evidence on ESG portfolio performance
Discuss regulatory developments (EU SFDR, SEC climate disclosure) and the greenwashing problem
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 28 - sections on individual tax considerations
Wilcox, Horvitz & DiBartolomeo, Investment Management for Taxable Private Investors (CFA Research Foundation)
MSCI ESG Research, Foundations of ESG Investing whitepaper
Larcker, Tayan & Watts, Seven Myths of ESG (Stanford Closer Look Series)

lecture_notes.introduction: Two themes have reshaped investment management in the last twenty years: tax awareness (driven by the spread of taxable accounts as wealth moved out of pensions into 401(k)s and individual brokerage accounts) and ESG / sustainable investing (driven by client demand and regulatory pressure). For taxable investors, the gap between pre-tax and after-tax returns can swallow more value than fund fees: a portfolio with 25% annual turnover at a 20% long-term capital gains rate loses roughly 1% per year to tax drag, dwarfing typical index fund expense ratios. Tax-aware investing — asset location, tax-loss harvesting, low-turnover indexing, charitable gifting of appreciated stock — is therefore the single largest controllable factor for high-net-worth individuals after asset allocation. ESG investing is more contested. The case for ESG ranges from values-based exclusion (no tobacco, no weapons) to risk-based integration (climate risk is financial risk) to impact investing (deploying capital where it produces measurable real-world outcomes). The empirical record on ESG performance is mixed: meta-analyses find roughly neutral risk-adjusted returns, with some evidence of outperformance from quality and low-volatility characteristics that often coincide with high ESG scores. Regulators have begun forcing disclosure of ESG methodologies (EU SFDR, SEC climate rule) to combat greenwashing. Today we cover both topics with the practical question: how do you build a portfolio that minimizes tax drag and reflects ESG values without sacrificing risk-adjusted returns?
key_concepts: Asset location: place tax-inefficient assets (taxable bonds, REITs, high-turnover funds) in tax-advantaged accounts (IRA, 401(k)) and tax-efficient assets (broad-market index funds, municipal bonds) in taxable accounts; can add 0.5-1.0% annually for high-net-worth investors
Tax-loss harvesting: realize capital losses to offset capital gains and up to $3,000 of ordinary income; reinvest in a similar but not 'substantially identical' security to avoid wash-sale rules
Long-term vs short-term capital gains: assets held > 1 year qualify for long-term capital gains rate (0/15/20% federal); short-term gains taxed as ordinary income (up to 37% federal)
Direct indexing: own the underlying stocks of an index in a separate account, allowing per-stock tax-loss harvesting and ESG customization; offered by Parametric, Aperio, Fidelity
ESG screening: exclude specific securities or industries (tobacco, weapons, fossil fuels) from the investable universe; the oldest form of values-based investing
ESG integration: incorporate ESG factors into traditional financial analysis as risk and opportunity inputs; does not exclude any sectors but adjusts weightings or expected returns
