Thematic / impact investing: actively invest in companies aligned with specific themes (clean energy, gender diversity, water) or that produce measurable real-world impact

ESG ratings divergence: the same company can receive radically different ESG scores from MSCI, Sustainalytics, and Refinitiv because each rater weights factors differently; correlation among ratings is roughly 0.5
Greenwashing: marketing a fund as ESG without substantive methodology; Morningstar removed roughly $1.4T in assets from its sustainable funds list in 2022 after applying stricter criteria

Performance evidence: Friede, Busch & Bassen 2015 meta-analysis of 2,200 studies found ESG had a positive or neutral impact on financial performance in roughly 90% of cases; more recent work finds the apparent ESG premium largely reflects exposure to quality and low-volatility factors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Construct two portfolios for a hypothetical $5M taxable client: a tax-naive portfolio (single brokerage account, all funds in one place) and a tax-aware portfolio (asset-located across taxable, IRA, and Roth IRA, with direct indexing in the taxable account for tax-loss harvesting). Project after-tax returns over 20 years assuming identical pre-tax returns. The tax-aware portfolio adds roughly 80 bps annually after tax, equivalent to ~17% more terminal wealth. Then walk through the construction of an ESG-tilted portfolio using the iShares MSCI USA ESG Select ETF (SUSA) versus the unscreened MSCI USA index.
setup_requirements: Tax bracket reference table (federal capital gains and ordinary income rates 2024)
Excel after-tax return projector with asset location decisions
MSCI ESG ratings methodology document
Friede-Busch-Bassen meta-analysis of ESG studies

review.value: For a high-net-worth taxable investor, tax inefficiency typically costs more than fund fees. Asset location and tax-loss harvesting can recover 50-100 bps annually — bigger than the typical fee differential between active and index funds

Tax-loss harvesting requires avoiding the same security (or 'substantially identical' securities) for 30 days. Replacing a sold S&P 500 ETF with a similar but distinct large-cap index ETF preserves the loss while keeping market exposure. The IRS enforces this strictly

Three investors who say 'I want ESG' may mean three completely different things: exclusionary screening, risk-integrated analysis, or impact investing. Clarify which definition before recommending products. The category is too broad to be useful without specification

review.key: ESG Is Not a Free Lunch
review.value: The empirical evidence on ESG performance is roughly neutral after controlling for factor exposures. ESG investing should be motivated by values or by genuine risk integration, not by an expectation of free outperformance

class_schedule.topic: Capstone: Building and Defending an Investment Thesis
learning_objectives: Synthesize asset allocation, security analysis, and behavioral discipline into a coherent investment process
Conduct a written and oral defense of a portfolio recommendation
Apply risk management and stress testing to a constructed portfolio
Reflect on the limits of knowledge and the role of humility in investment management
readings: Bodie, Kane & Marcus, Investments (12th ed.), Ch. 27 - The Theory of Active Portfolio Management
Graham, The Intelligent Investor (rev. ed.), Ch. 20 - Margin of Safety as the Central Concept
Bernstein, Against the Gods, Ch. 18-19 - The legacy of probability

lecture_notes.introduction: We have spent fourteen sessions building the conceptual and quantitative toolkit of modern investment management: asset classes, risk and return, portfolio theory, CAPM, multifactor models, bond pricing, equity valuation, behavioral finance, the active-passive debate, performance measurement, asset allocation, alternatives, tax awareness, and ESG. This final session is about putting it all together into a coherent investment process that you can actually defend in front of a client, an investment committee, or your own future self. The world's greatest investors share a common attribute: a disciplined process that is documented, repeatable, and survives both good and bad markets. Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway annual letter (read every one — they are the best free investment education in the world) is a public record of a consistent process applied for sixty years. Howard Marks's memos at Oaktree are similarly disciplined. David Swensen's investment policy at Yale was written and followed for decades. What unites these investors is not a magic stock pick or a market timing call but a process they trusted enough to follow when markets were panicking. Today we work through the elements of an investment thesis: the objective, the universe, the analytical approach, the construction, the risk controls, and the review cadence. You will then present your final project portfolio to the class and defend it.
key_concepts: Investment process: a written, repeatable approach to generating ideas, analyzing them, sizing positions, monitoring, and selling
Investment thesis: a clear statement of why you own a security (or asset class) including the catalysts you expect, the risks you accept, and the conditions that would invalidate the thesis
Pre-mortem analysis: imagine the investment has failed badly and write the post-mortem now; identifies risks that optimism hides
Position sizing: Kelly criterion for theoretically optimal sizing; risk parity, equal weight, conviction weighting more common in practice
Risk budgeting: allocate a fixed amount of total portfolio risk across strategies or positions, ensuring no single bet dominates
Stress testing: simulate the portfolio's performance in historical crisis scenarios (1987 crash, 2000 dotcom, 2008 GFC, 2020 COVID, 2022 rate shock) and forward-looking scenarios
Maximum drawdown: largest peak-to-trough decline; investors must be able to tolerate the maximum drawdown of their strategy or they will sell at the worst time
Sell discipline: rules for when to exit a position (price target reached, thesis broken, better opportunity available, risk-control triggered); often the weakest part of an investment process
Documentation: a written investment memo for each major position; future-self protection against revisionist reasoning
Humility: the best investors hold their views with appropriate strength and update on new evidence; the worst hold strong views weakly justified and refuse to revise
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through Warren Buffett's 1965 Berkshire Hathaway annual letter when he took over the failing textile company, then his 1985 letter announcing the wind-down of the textile operations after twenty years of trying. Read passages aloud demonstrating his honesty about failure ('I made the wrong decision') and his clarity about why. Then walk through Howard Marks's 'I Beg to Differ' memo from July 2022 in which he laid out his contrarian case for credit despite recession fears. Finally, walk through a brief student investment thesis for a hypothetical position and defend it against challenges from the class about correlation, duration, and tax efficiency.
setup_requirements: Berkshire Hathaway annual letters from berkshirehathaway.com
Howard Marks memos from oaktreecapital.com
Final project portfolio presentations from each student
Whiteboard or projector for thesis defense

review.value: You cannot predict markets reliably. You can build a process that produces good outcomes on average and survives bad markets. Process is what you have control over; outcomes are partly luck
Every investment should have a written thesis: why you own it, what could prove you wrong, and at what point you will sell. Without this, future-you will rationalize past mistakes and never learn

Historical scenarios are useful but insufficient. The 2008 GFC was unprecedented in many ways. Imagine combinations the past has not seen (rates rising while equities crash, dollar collapse, deglobalization shock) and ask whether your portfolio survives them

The best investors are intellectually humble: they know what they do not know, they update on new evidence, and they admit mistakes. Overconfidence is the dominant cognitive failure in investment management. Humility is rare and therefore valuable

questions.question: A portfolio is 60% invested in a stock fund with E[R]=10%, sigma=18%, and 40% invested in a bond fund with E[R]=5%, sigma=8%. The correlation between the two funds is 0.20. Compute (a) the portfolio's expected return, (b) the portfolio's variance, (c) the portfolio's standard deviation, and (d) the diversification benefit relative to a weighted average of the standard deviations.
questions.answer:
(a) E[Rp] = 0.6*10% + 0.4*5% = 6.0% + 2.0% = 8.0%

(b) Variance = (0.6)^2*(0.18)^2 + (0.4)^2*(0.08)^2 + 2*(0.6)*(0.4)*0.20*(0.18)*(0.08)
= 0.36*0.0324 + 0.16*0.0064 + 2*0.24*0.20*0.0144
= 0.011664 + 0.001024 + 0.0013824
= 0.0140704

(c) Standard deviation = sqrt(0.0140704) = 0.11862, or 11.86%

(d) Weighted average of standard deviations = 0.6*18% + 0.4*8% = 14.0%
Diversification benefit = 14.0% - 11.86% = 2.14 percentage points reduction in risk. The 2.14 percentage point risk reduction comes from the imperfect correlation (0.20) between the two funds.

rubric.key: Correct expected return calculation (8.0%)
Correct variance calculation with all three terms
Correct standard deviation (11.86%)
Correct diversification benefit calculation and interpretation

questions.question: An investor reports that her portfolio earned the following annual returns over the past five years: +30%, -20%, +25%, -15%, +20%. (a) Compute the arithmetic mean annual return. (b) Compute the geometric mean annual return. (c) Explain in one paragraph why these two measures differ and which is more appropriate for describing the investor's actual realized growth.
questions.answer:
(a) Arithmetic mean = (0.30 - 0.20 + 0.25 - 0.15 + 0.20) / 5 = 0.40 / 5 = 0.08, or 8.0% per year.

(b) Geometric mean: compound growth of $1 over 5 years.
Ending wealth = (1.30)*(0.80)*(1.25)*(0.85)*(1.20) = 1.30*0.80 = 1.04; 1.04*1.25 = 1.30; 1.30*0.85 = 1.105; 1.105*1.20 = 1.326
Geometric mean = (1.326)^(1/5) - 1 = 1.0581 - 1 = 0.0581, or 5.81% per year.

(c) The arithmetic mean (8.0%) and geometric mean (5.81%) differ by 2.19 percentage points because of volatility drag: large losses require larger gains to recover, and the arithmetic average ignores this asymmetry. A 50% loss requires a 100% gain to break even, not a 50% gain. The geometric mean correctly reflects what $1 actually compounded to ($1.326 over five years), while the arithmetic mean overstates compound growth. For describing realized historical returns the geometric mean is correct; for forward-looking expectations under i.i.d. assumptions the arithmetic mean is correct. When in doubt about historical reporting, use the geometric mean.

rubric.key: Correct arithmetic mean calculation (8.0%)
Correct geometric mean calculation (5.81%)
Correct explanation of volatility drag and which measure is appropriate when

questions.question: An investor is considering two assets: Asset A has E[R]=12%, sigma=20%; Asset B has E[R]=8%, sigma=12%. The risk-free rate is 3%. (a) Which asset has the higher Sharpe ratio? (b) The investor wants to construct a portfolio with 14% volatility using only Asset A and the risk-free asset. What weight should be placed on Asset A? (c) What is the expected return of this portfolio?
questions.answer:
(a) Sharpe ratio for Asset A = (12% - 3%) / 20% = 0.45
Sharpe ratio for Asset B = (8% - 3%) / 12% = 0.4167
Asset A has the higher Sharpe ratio (0.45 vs 0.4167), so it offers better risk-adjusted return.

(b) For a portfolio combining Asset A (sigma=20%) and the risk-free asset (sigma=0), the portfolio standard deviation is simply w_A * sigma_A:
14% = w_A * 20%
w_A = 14/20 = 0.70, or 70% in Asset A and 30% in the risk-free asset.

(c) Portfolio expected return = w_A * E[R_A] + (1 - w_A) * Rf = 0.70 * 12% + 0.30 * 3% = 8.4% + 0.9% = 9.3%.

Verification: Sharpe ratio of the resulting portfolio = (9.3 - 3)/14 = 0.45, the same as Asset A's Sharpe ratio (as it must be on the CAL).

rubric.key: Correct Sharpe ratio calculations and identification of higher
Correct weight calculation for target volatility
Correct expected return and verification via Sharpe ratio invariance

questions.question: A stock has beta of 1.3. The risk-free rate is 4.0% and the expected return on the market is 9.5%. (a) Use the CAPM to compute the required return on this stock. (b) The stock is expected to earn 12% next year based on analyst estimates. According to CAPM, is the stock underpriced or overpriced? (c) If the stock's beta were 0.7 instead, what would be the CAPM required return, and how would your answer to (b) change?
questions.answer:
(a) Market risk premium = E[Rm] - Rf = 9.5% - 4.0% = 5.5%
CAPM required return = Rf + beta * MRP = 4.0% + 1.3 * 5.5% = 4.0% + 7.15% = 11.15%

(b) Expected return (12%) > Required return (11.15%), so the stock is offering more return than CAPM says is needed for its risk. This means the stock is currently UNDERPRICED — its expected return exceeds the risk-adjusted hurdle rate by 0.85%. Buying it at the current price implies positive alpha of 0.85%.

(c) With beta 0.7:
Required return = 4.0% + 0.7 * 5.5% = 4.0% + 3.85% = 7.85%
Expected return (12%) is now far above required return (7.85%), so the stock would be even more underpriced — alpha of 4.15%. Lower beta means lower required return for the same level of expected return, so the stock looks more attractive.

rubric.key: Correct CAPM application with beta 1.3 and identification of under/overpricing
Correct recomputation with beta 0.7
Correct interpretation that lower beta produces a more attractive risk-adjusted comparison

questions.question: You run a four-factor regression (Carhart) for a mutual fund and obtain the following coefficients (all statistically significant): intercept (alpha) = 0.15% per month, beta_market = 1.05, beta_SMB = 0.42, beta_HML = -0.31, beta_MOM = 0.28. (a) Describe the fund's style in plain language. (b) Annualize the alpha and discuss whether this represents skill or luck. (c) Why would a sophisticated allocator prefer this multifactor view over a simple comparison to the S&P 500?
questions.answer:
(a) Style description:
- Beta_market = 1.05: very slightly more volatile than the market
- Beta_SMB = 0.42: significant tilt toward small-cap stocks (positive loading on Small Minus Big)
- Beta_HML = -0.31: moderate tilt toward growth stocks (negative loading on High Minus Low book-to-market means it favors low B/M, i.e., growth)
- Beta_MOM = 0.28: meaningful momentum tilt, owns stocks that have been recent outperformers

In plain language: the fund is a small-cap growth fund with a momentum tilt and approximately market-level exposure to broad equity risk.

(b) Annualizing the alpha: 0.15% per month * 12 = 1.8% per year. After controlling for market, size, value, and momentum exposures, the fund delivered 1.8% annual excess return. Whether this is skill or luck requires further evidence: statistical significance over a long sample (typically 5+ years monthly), consistency across sub-periods, absence of additional systematic exposures the four-factor model misses (quality, low-vol, profitability), and accounting for fees if the alpha is gross. A statistically significant positive alpha over 10+ years across factor models is rare and suggests genuine skill.

(c) A sophisticated allocator prefers the multifactor view because it separates style exposure (which can be replicated cheaply with smart beta ETFs) from genuine skill (which deserves a higher fee). It avoids paying active fees for what is effectively a small-cap growth tilt. It enables apples-to-apples comparison across managers with different style tilts.

rubric.key: Correct interpretation of all four factor loadings into a coherent style description
Correct annualization and thoughtful discussion of skill vs luck criteria
Clear explanation of why multifactor analysis matters for fund selection

questions.question: A pension fund holds a portfolio with beta of 1.2 against the market. The chief investment officer wants to reduce the portfolio's beta to 0.9 without selling any of the underlying securities (to avoid taxes and trading costs). Describe two distinct ways to achieve a beta of 0.9, and explain the trade-offs.
questions.answer:
Method 1: Combine the existing portfolio with the risk-free asset.
The portfolio of weight w in the existing fund and (1-w) in cash has beta = w * 1.2. To achieve beta = 0.9, set w * 1.2 = 0.9, so w = 0.75. Allocate 75% of the fund to the existing portfolio and 25% to short-term Treasuries. This avoids selling any underlying securities but requires either holding new cash or actually selling some securities to fund the cash allocation.

Method 2: Short S&P 500 index futures.
The pension can sell index futures sufficient to reduce effective beta. If the portfolio has $1B in equities at beta 1.2, the dollar beta is $1.2B. To reach effective beta 0.9, dollar beta should be $0.9B, requiring a short of $0.3B in S&P 500 exposure. At S&P = 5000, each E-mini contract is $250,000 of exposure, so $0.3B requires 1,200 short contracts. Trade-offs: requires margin posting and daily mark-to-market; introduces basis risk; rolling futures incurs roll costs; may have tax implications under Section 1256 (60/40 long/short capital gains treatment for index futures).

Method 1 is simpler operationally but requires fresh cash. Method 2 is more efficient and is the institutional standard.

rubric.key: Method 1 correctly described with weight calculation
Method 2 correctly described with futures sizing logic
Trade-offs of each method clearly articulated

quizzes.topic: Bond Pricing, Duration, and Equity Valuation

questions.question: A 10-year corporate bond has a 5% annual coupon, face value $1,000, and currently trades at $1,082.00. (a) Compute the bond's current yield. (b) Compute the bond's yield-to-maturity (YTM). (c) Is the bond trading at a premium or a discount, and explain the relationship between coupon rate and YTM that determines this.
questions.answer:
(a) Current yield = annual coupon / current price = $50 / $1,082 = 0.0462, or 4.62%.

(b) YTM is the y that solves: 1,082 = sum_{t=1}^{10} 50/(1+y)^t + 1000/(1+y)^10

At y = 4%: PV = 50 * [1 - (1.04)^-10] / 0.04 + 1000/(1.04)^10 = 50 * 8.1109 + 675.56 = 405.55 + 675.56 = 1081.11
So YTM is approximately 4.00%. Using Excel YIELD confirms YTM near 4.00%.

(c) The bond trades at a premium ($1,082 > $1,000 face value) because the coupon rate (5%) is higher than the YTM (4%). When a bond's coupon rate exceeds the market yield, the bond's promised cash flows are richer than what the market currently demands for that risk and maturity, so investors bid the price above par. The premium amortizes toward face value as the bond approaches maturity. The current yield (4.62%) overstates the actual return because it ignores this premium amortization.

rubric.key: Correct current yield calculation
Correct YTM calculation (4.0%) with method shown
Correct identification of premium and explanation of coupon vs yield relationship

questions.question: A bond has Macaulay duration of 7.4 years and a yield-to-maturity of 5.0%. (a) Compute the modified duration. (b) Estimate the percentage price change if yields rise by 80 basis points. (c) The bond's convexity is 65. Compute the more accurate price change using the duration-plus-convexity approximation. Explain why the convexity adjustment matters for larger yield changes.
questions.answer:
(a) Modified duration = 7.4 / 1.05 = 7.048 years.

(b) Duration-only: dP/P = -7.048 * 0.0080 = -0.0564, or -5.64%.

(c) Duration-plus-convexity: dP/P = -7.048 * 0.0080 + 0.5 * 65 * (0.0080)^2 = -0.0564 + 0.00208 = -0.05432, or -5.43%.

The more accurate estimate is a 5.43% price decline rather than 5.64%. The convexity adjustment adds back 0.21 percentage points because the bond's true price-yield relationship is curved (convex), not linear. Duration-only estimates always overstate price declines and understate price gains. For small yield changes the convexity adjustment is tiny; for larger changes (100+ bps like 2022) the convexity adjustment becomes material. Higher convexity is desirable because it makes the bond gain more on rate drops and lose less on rate rises.

rubric.key: Correct modified duration calculation
Correct duration-only price change estimate
Correct duration-plus-convexity calculation
Correct explanation of why convexity matters for larger yield changes

questions.question: A mature consumer staples company is expected to pay a dividend of $3.00 next year. Dividends are expected to grow at 4% per year forever. The investor's required return on equity is 9%. (a) Use the Gordon growth model to compute intrinsic value per share. (b) The stock currently trades at $52. Is it overvalued, undervalued, or fairly valued? (c) Suppose the analyst's growth assumption falls from 4% to 3%. What is the new intrinsic value, and what does this teach about the sensitivity of DDM to inputs?
questions.answer:
(a) Gordon growth: P0 = D1 / (r - g) = 3.00 / (0.09 - 0.04) = 3.00 / 0.05 = $60.00 per share.

(b) Intrinsic value ($60) > Market price ($52), so the stock is undervalued by $8 per share, or about 13.3%.

(c) With g = 3%: P0 = 3.00 / (0.09 - 0.03) = 3.00 / 0.06 = $50.00 per share.

The intrinsic value drops from $60 to $50, a decline of $10 (16.7%) for a 1 percentage point change in the growth assumption. This demonstrates DDM input sensitivity: small differences in growth assumptions produce large differences in valuation. The denominator (r-g) is small and any change to it has outsized effect.

Lessons: always present DDM/DCF outputs as a sensitivity range; bound terminal growth at or below long-run economic growth (4-5% nominal); triangulate with multiples; apply the margin of safety principle to protect against estimation error.

rubric.key: Correct Gordon growth calculation with g = 4%
Correct identification of undervaluation with magnitude
Correct recalculation with g = 3%
Insightful discussion of input sensitivity and practical implications

quizzes.topic: Behavioral Finance, Active vs Passive, and Performance Measurement

questions.question: A retail investor logs into her brokerage account weekly. Over the past year she has sold five winning positions to 'lock in gains' (each had risen 15-25%) and held seven losing positions because 'they will come back' (each is down 20-40%). Identify the specific behavioral biases at work, name the well-known phenomenon this pattern is called, and explain why it is financially destructive.
questions.answer:
Biases at work:
1) Loss aversion (Kahneman-Tversky): the pain of realizing a loss feels roughly 2x the pleasure of an equivalent gain.
2) Reference-dependent thinking: she anchors to her purchase price rather than current intrinsic value.
3) Confirmation bias: she seeks reasons the losing positions will recover and ignores contrary evidence.
4) Mental accounting: each position is treated as a separate bucket.

Name: the disposition effect (Shefrin and Statman, 1985) — selling winners too early and holding losers too long.

Why destructive:
1) Tax inefficiency: in a taxable account, selling winners realizes gains (tax bill), while holding losers prevents tax-loss harvesting. The IRS-optimal behavior is the OPPOSITE: harvest losses, defer gains.
2) Empirical evidence (Odean, 1998): the winners disposition-affected investors sold outperformed the losers they kept by ~3.4% over the next year.
3) Distortion of position sizing: losing positions become a larger share of the portfolio over time.
4) Erosion of decision quality: anchoring to purchase price prevents the correct question — 'given current information, would I buy this today?'

The defense is process: pre-commit sell rules, automatic rebalancing, and tax-loss harvesting that overrides the emotional impulse.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies loss aversion and at least two other biases
Correctly names the disposition effect
Explains tax inefficiency consequence
Explains empirical underperformance evidence (Odean)
Recommends process-based defense

questions.question: Two equity funds are evaluated over the same 5-year period. Fund X: 11.5% return, 16% volatility, beta 1.10, tracking error vs S&P 500 = 5%. Fund Y: 9.0% return, 11% volatility, beta 0.75, tracking error = 4%. Risk-free rate 3%, market return 9%. (a) Compute Sharpe, Treynor, Jensen's alpha, and information ratio for each. (b) Which fund is 'better,' and why does the answer depend on the investor's situation?
questions.answer:
(a) Calculations:

Fund X:
Sharpe = (11.5 - 3) / 16 = 0.531
Treynor = (11.5 - 3) / 1.10 = 7.73
Jensen's alpha = 11.5 - [3 + 1.10 * (9 - 3)] = 11.5 - 9.6 = 1.9%
Information ratio = (11.5 - 9) / 5 = 0.50

Fund Y:
Sharpe = (9.0 - 3) / 11 = 0.545
Treynor = (9.0 - 3) / 0.75 = 8.00
Jensen's alpha = 9.0 - [3 + 0.75 * (9 - 3)] = 9.0 - 7.5 = 1.5%
Information ratio = (9.0 - 9) / 4 = 0.00

(b) Which is better depends on the fund's role:
- If this is the investor's only risky asset: Use Sharpe. Fund Y is slightly better (0.545 vs 0.531).
- If this is one sleeve of a diversified portfolio: Use Treynor. Fund Y is better (8.00 vs 7.73).
- If the investor cares about active management skill against the S&P 500: Use Jensen's alpha and IR. Fund X is meaningfully better (alpha 1.9% vs 1.5%; IR 0.50 vs 0.00).

The answer is 'Fund X if you want active management beyond the market; Fund Y if you want defensive equity exposure.' This is why sophisticated allocators use multiple metrics.

rubric.key: All four metrics correctly computed for both funds
Correct identification that the answer depends on the fund's role
Correct mapping of metric to investor situation

questions.question: Over the past 20 years, the SPIVA U.S. Scorecard has shown that approximately 96% of large-cap U.S. equity active mutual funds underperformed the S&P 500 net of fees. Yet active management still attracts trillions of dollars. Provide three possible explanations for this apparent paradox and assess which is most credible.
questions.answer:
1) Behavioral / cognitive: Overconfidence bias (investors believe THEIR fund manager will be in the 4% who outperform), recency bias, and survivorship bias. Marketing exploits these biases. Commission-based advisors have incentives to recommend active funds.

2) Structural / advisory: Much active fund AUM is in retirement plans where employees did not choose the funds — the plan sponsor did, often guided by recordkeeper relationships. Many investors do not know SPIVA exists. Wirehouse and bank channels favor active funds because of revenue-sharing arrangements.

3) Niche skill / non-equity rationale: Not all active management is in large-cap U.S. equity. In distressed debt, frontier markets, and micro-cap the case for active is stronger. Some investors hold 'active' funds for ESG, factor tilts, or tax management mandates that pure index funds cannot deliver.

Most credible: A combination of (1) and (2). Behavioral biases plus structural distribution channels explain most persistence. The evidence has been public for two decades. The continued shift toward index funds shows the market is gradually correcting, but slowly. The niche-skill explanation has some merit but does not explain the bulk of active AUM in mainstream equity funds.

rubric.key: Three distinct credible explanations with substance
Reasoned assessment of which is most credible with supporting argument

quizzes.topic: Asset Allocation, Alternatives, and Tax-Aware ESG Investing

questions.question: A 30-year-old engineer earning $120,000 per year wants to start a long-term retirement portfolio. She has $50,000 in savings and expects to add $20,000 per year. She has high risk tolerance and a 35-year time horizon. (a) Recommend a strategic asset allocation. (b) Recommend specific low-cost ETFs. (c) Recommend a rebalancing rule and justify it.
questions.answer:
(a) Strategic asset allocation:
- 65% U.S. equities (broad market, market-cap weighted)
- 20% International developed markets equities
- 5% Emerging markets equities
- 7% Intermediate-term U.S. bonds
- 3% U.S. REITs

Total: 90% equities, 7% bonds, 3% real estate. Rationale: 35-year horizon means equity volatility can be ridden out; high risk tolerance and long horizon argue for heavy equity weight. International diversification reduces single-country risk. Small bond allocation provides psychological insurance to avoid panic selling.

(b) ETF implementations:
- Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (VTI), expense ratio 0.03%
- Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets ETF (VEA), 0.05%
- Vanguard FTSE Emerging Markets ETF (VWO), 0.08%
- Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND), 0.03%
- Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), 0.12%

Weighted average expense ratio: ~0.04%, dramatically below typical active funds.

(c) Rebalancing rule: Annual rebalancing on her birthday with a 5% threshold tolerance. Use new contributions to rebalance with no selling at all (no taxes). Annual plus threshold combines the psychological discipline of a calendar trigger with the cost efficiency of trading only when meaningful drift has occurred.

rubric.key: Reasonable strategic allocation appropriate for the investor profile
Specific low-cost ETF recommendations with expense ratios
Rebalancing rule with cost-aware justification

questions.question: An institutional allocator is being pitched a private equity fund with 'historical returns of 17% IRR over 10 years' versus 9% for the S&P 500 over the same period. Identify at least four reasons this comparison may overstate the true risk-adjusted advantage of the PE fund.
questions.answer:
1) Leverage: LBO funds use 60-80% debt financing. The S&P 500 is unlevered. Adjusting PE returns for leverage shrinks the apparent outperformance.

2) Illiquidity premium: PE locks up capital for 8-12 years. The illiquidity should command a 3-5% annual premium just as compensation. Deducting this, much of the excess return disappears.

3) Smoothing / stale pricing: Private equity NAVs are appraisal-based and updated quarterly with lags. This artificially smooths reported returns and understates true volatility. Risk-adjusted measures are biased upward.

4) IRR vs TWR distortion: IRR weighted by cash flow timing differs from time-weighted return. Early successful exits inflate IRR even if the unrealized portion subsequently underperforms. The 'public market equivalent' (PME) metric by Kaplan and Schoar provides a fairer comparison.

5) Selection bias: The pitched fund has strong historical returns; the universe includes many failures. Top- vs bottom-quartile dispersion is typically 1500-2000 bps annually.

6) Fee structure: PE fees are 2 and 20 plus other expenses totaling 5-7% per year gross.

7) Vintage year cherry-picking: 10-year PE windows ending in 2024 include the strong 2010-2014 post-GFC vintages.

A defensible comparison would use the Kaplan-Schoar PME net of all fees, adjusted for leverage and illiquidity.

rubric.key: Identifies leverage adjustment issue
Identifies illiquidity premium / smoothing issue
Identifies IRR vs TWR / PME issue
Identifies selection bias or vintage year issue

questions.question: A high-net-worth investor in the 37% federal tax bracket (plus 3.8% NIIT) holds $2M in taxable accounts and $500K in IRAs. Currently both accounts hold 60% S&P 500 ETF and 40% intermediate-term taxable bond ETF. Recommend a tax-aware reorganization and explain the after-tax improvement.
questions.answer:
Apply asset location to put the most tax-inefficient assets in the IRA and tax-efficient assets in the taxable account.

Current setup (both accounts 60/40): Taxable holds $1.2M equities + $0.8M bonds; IRA holds $0.3M equities + $0.2M bonds. Total: $1.5M equities, $1.0M bonds.

Problem: Bonds in taxable generate ordinary-income interest taxed at 37% + 3.8% NIIT = 40.8% combined. The S&P 500 ETF generates qualified dividends (23.8%) and unrealized capital gains (deferred). Bonds are tax-inefficient; equities are tax-efficient.

Reorganized (preserving household 60/40 via munis):
- Taxable ($2M): $1.5M equities (VTI/ITOT) + $0.5M municipal bonds (tax-exempt at federal level).
- IRA ($500K): $0.3M taxable bonds + $0.2M REITs (REITs distribute most income as ordinary income, so sheltering is valuable).

Household mix preserved at 60/40 but each asset is located where it is most tax-efficient.

Quantitative improvement: Asset location for a portfolio of this size in the top bracket typically adds 50-100 bps per year of after-tax return. On $2.5M, that is $12,500-$25,000 per year, or roughly $400,000-$800,000 over 25 years through compounding. Additional layered techniques: tax-loss harvesting (40-100 bps more), charitable giving of appreciated stock, and keeping turnover low.

rubric.key: Correct identification of which assets are tax-efficient vs tax-inefficient
Specific reorganization preserving overall household allocation
Quantitative estimate of after-tax improvement with reasonable magnitude

major_assignments.description: Build a complete portfolio optimization model in Excel or Python that takes historical returns for ten asset classes, computes the efficient frontier, identifies the minimum variance and tangency portfolios under realistic constraints (long-only, position limits), and produces a written investment recommendation for a representative individual investor. course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce the participation grade. Capstone presentation attendance is mandatory.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without documented emergency.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts and problem-solving approaches is encouraged. All written work, models, and analyses must be individual and original. Cite any external data sources or research used.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying valuation models, code, or written analysis from the internet, prior students, or AI tools without disclosure constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Excel (with Solver add-in enabled), access to a financial data source (Yahoo Finance, FRED, or Bloomberg if available), and optionally Python with pandas, numpy, and statsmodels for the more advanced quantitative work.
